item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whereas kilocalories in food energy are usually shortened to just "calories" in everyday speech, it's not something that should be done in an article.
Because

additional requirement of approximately 4600 calories

that would be equal to 3 cedar nuts or maybe a couple of M&Ms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, the guy was dumped and as a result he wrote the books? I didn't get that from the story at all. If that's I was supposed to get from it, it wasn't obvious enough for me :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting for the implication that he turned into the books.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastically written, definitely a +1, but you have the SCP listed as euclid. The beings created by the SCP are euclid, but the objects themselves are technically safe as long as no one touches them.

Possible change?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, what I liked the most WAS the disconnection.
Instead of a cliche love story, we get the effect to BE related to the concept without being the classic reaction.
He felt empty. The SCP makes others empty. And where does that extra energy go?
Is his 'soul' trying to fill the 'void' literally?

+1, specially for that one tht made more of itself in an attempt of escaping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, on the other hand, found the linked elements natural and effective. A-plus skip here, floated my upboat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should know that I share your distaste for body horror skips, so you had an uphill battle with me here.

There are three components to this SCP, mechanically, that I gleaned from reading it — I might be missing something.

  1. Christian Paulman, a barista/author
  2. The book/fad diet
  3. The creatures themselves

I have to admit that, while I see the explicit connection between these three features (Paulman gets dumped by a girl/struggles with mental illness, writes a fad diet book, fad diet book makes people spoopy), I have to admit the article isn't communicating the implict reason why to me properly. And I'm also afraid that I kind of don't like the "heartbreaking woman as the cause of a man's downfall" sort of story, admittedly, which is all I'm really gleaning from this backstory.

-1, but I hope you know it comes from a place of deep respect for your work. Positives: Excellent, breezy technical writing and good image choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've said before that I can't stand body horror skips, so I thought I'd try to overcome that (and maybe come to understand them a little better) by doing one of my own.

The image is creative commons from the visual records collection. Special thanks to several folks who helped me with this on IRC.
